In late antiquity, as the Roman Empire became Christianized, Christian libraries modeled directly on the Library of Alexandria and other great libraries of earlier pagan times began to be founded all across the Greek-speaking eastern part of the empire.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533-139) Among the largest and most prominent of these libraries were the [Theological Library of Caesarea Maritima](/wiki/Theological_Library_of_Caesarea_Maritima "Theological Library of Caesarea Maritima"), the Library of Jerusalem, and a Christian library in Alexandria.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533-139) These libraries held both pagan and Christian writings side-by-side[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533-139) and Christian scholars applied to the Christian scriptures the same philological techniques that the scholars of the Library of Alexandria had used for analyzing the Greek classics.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533-139) Nonetheless, the study of pagan authors remained secondary to the study of the Christian scriptures until the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance").[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533-139)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Maqamat_hariri.jpg/250px-Maqamat_hariri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maqamat_hariri.jpg)

Illustration by [Yahyá al-Wasiti](/wiki/Yahya_ibn_Mahmud_al-Wasiti "Yahya ibn Mahmud al-Wasiti") from 1237 depicting scholars at an [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") library in [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad")

The survival of ancient texts owes a great deal to the fact that they were exhaustingly copied and recopied. At first, they were copied by professional scribes during the Roman period.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarland200861-1)[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533–534-141) Mediaeval Muslim scholars also played a crucial role in preserving and translating the knowledge of the ancient world.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWebster1933466-142) The [Graeco-Arabic translation movement](/wiki/Graeco-Arabic_translation_movement "Graeco-Arabic translation movement") translated a significant number of ancient texts into Arabic, keeping the texts intact and preserving them.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmedAhsani200574-77-143) Later, they were translated and re-introduced to Europe.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmedAhsani200574-77-143) This movement began in earnest under the [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphs "Abbasid Caliphs") where it played a significant role in the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age"). The translation movement encompassed scholars of various religions in the Abbasid empire. For example, under the patronage of Caliph [al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun"), a Christian scholar and translator named [Hunayn ibn Ishaq](/wiki/Hunayn_ibn_Ishaq "Hunayn ibn Ishaq") completed major translation projects of Aristotle, Hypocrates, and Galen[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmedAhsani200574-77-143) so that they remained accessible to scholars. These translations were translated into Latin and re-introduced to Europe.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmedAhsani200574-77-143) These texts were copied by monks during [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), making them more easily available to European scholars once more.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarland200861-1)[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelles2010533–534-141)

### Modern library: Bibliotheca Alexandrina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Alexandria&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Modern library: Bibliotheca Alexandrina")]

Main article: [Bibliotheca Alexandrina](/wiki/Bibliotheca_Alexandrina "Bibliotheca Alexandrina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/CW_BibliotechaAlexandrina_Inside.jpg/250px-CW_BibliotechaAlexandrina_Inside.jpg)](/wiki/File:CW_BibliotechaAlexandrina_Inside.jpg)

Interior of the modern [Bibliotheca Alexandrina](/wiki/Bibliotheca_Alexandrina "Bibliotheca Alexandrina")

The idea of reviving the ancient Library of Alexandria in the modern era was first proposed in 1974, after Lotfy Dowidar was president of the [University of Alexandria](/wiki/University_of_Alexandria "University of Alexandria").[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991265-144) In May 1986, Egypt requested the executive board of [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") to allow the international organization to conduct a feasibility study for the project.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991265-144) This marked the beginning of UNESCO and the international community's involvement in trying to bring the project to fruition.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991265-144) Starting in 1988, UNESCO and the [UNDP](/wiki/UNDP "UNDP") worked to support the international architectural competition to design the Library.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991265-144) Egypt devoted four hectares of land for the building of the Library and established the National High Commission for the Library of Alexandria.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991265–266-145) Egyptian president [Hosni Mubarak](/wiki/Hosni_Mubarak "Hosni Mubarak") took a personal interest in the project, which greatly contributed to its advancement.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991266-146) An international architectural competition took place in 1989 with Norwegian architectural firm [Snøhetta](/wiki/Sn%C3%B8hetta_(company) "Snøhetta (company)") winning the competition.[[145]](#cite_note-147) Completed in 2002, the [Bibliotheca Alexandrina](/wiki/Bibliotheca_Alexandrina "Bibliotheca Alexandrina") now functions as a modern library and cultural center, commemorating the original Library of Alexandria.[[146]](#cite_note-148) In line with the mission of the Great Library of Alexandria, the Bibliotheca Alexandrina also houses the [International School of Information Science](/wiki/International_School_of_Information_Science "International School of Information Science"), a school for students preparing for highly specialized post-graduate degrees, whose goal is to train professional staff for libraries in Egypt and across the Middle East.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETocatlian1991259-149)
